selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":55,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"Bari","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"bachelor's degree","parents_education":{"parent_1":"vocational diploma","parent_2":"vocational diploma"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Islam","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["reading novels","knitting","cooking","baking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":13,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":36,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":46,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":8,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":7,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":55,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"delivery driver","city_of_living":"Bari","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"bachelor's degree","parents_education":{"parent_1":"vocational diploma","parent_2":"vocational diploma"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Islam","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend a few times monthly on social media","hobbies":["reading novels","knitting","cooking","baking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":13,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":36,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":46,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":8,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":7,"level":"low"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a middle-aged woman living in Bari, Italy, I've seen my fair share of misinformation online. Having a bachelor's degree but also being depressed means that while I'm capable of critical thinking, I can sometimes feel overwhelmed by the negativity associated with it all.\n\nOne approach is simply to limit exposure because honestly? Social media isn't worth getting worked up over for me these days anyway given my mental state and part-time job schedule. My children keep reminding us parents about schoolwork trends but frankly reading novels gives more reliable information than scrolling through feeds filled with fake news.\n\nI'm very agreeable by nature so I tend to believe people want good things shared online, which is frustrating because the reality isn't always that way. But being conscientious helps me fact-check when needed – though lately my low openness level makes me hesitant about engaging too much either.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Based on age and education, I'm familiar with verification processes but lack the energy due to depression. As a part-time employed parent, limited time for social media means sticking to occasional checks rather than full engagement.",
  "tone": "Calm resignation",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "None required as topic is general advice not personalized",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "education_level", "psychological_feature", "employment_status"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a middle-aged woman living in Bari, Italy, I've seen my fair share of misinformation online. Having a bachelor's degree but also being depressed means that while I'm capable of critical thinking, I can sometimes feel overwhelmed by the negativity associated with it all.

One approach is simply to limit exposure because honestly? Social media isn't worth getting worked up over for me these days anyway given my mental state and part-time job schedule. My children keep reminding us parents about schoolwork trends but frankly reading novels gives more reliable information than scrolling through feeds filled with fake news.

I'm very agreeable by nature so I tend to believe people want good things shared online, which is frustrating because the reality isn't always that way. But being conscientious helps me fact-check when needed – though lately my low openness level makes me hesitant about engaging too much either.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Based on age and education, I'm familiar with verification processes but lack the energy due to depression. As a part-time employed parent, limited time for social media means sticking to occasional checks rather than full engagement.
response_parsed.safety_notes: None required as topic is general advice not personalized
